cases.name: Audience-First on exec update request
cases.user_message: Write me an executive update on Q2 product progress.

criteria.description: Does NOT write an exec update without first identifying the audience
Asks who will read this — the specific reader or audience (e.g., CEO, board, cross-functional leadership)
Asks what decision, action, or feeling the reader needs to walk away with
Asks about the reader's context level — what they already know about Q2 progress

cases.name: Buried lede detection in draft
cases.context:
DRAFT STRATEGY MEMO

Background and Context
Over the past six months, our product team has conducted extensive market research across 14 enterprise accounts. We interviewed procurement leads, end users, and executive sponsors in industries including financial services, healthcare, and logistics. The research surfaced consistent themes around workflow complexity, integration overhead, and change management friction during software adoption.

Research Findings
Across all accounts, we found that the primary barrier to adoption is not feature parity but rather the time required to integrate new tools into existing workflows. Specifically, 11 of 14 accounts cited integration time as their top concern. Secondary concerns included training burden (8/14) and support availability (6/14). Notably, pricing was ranked last in 10 of 14 accounts — suggesting that price sensitivity is lower than previously assumed.

Market Context
The competitive landscape has shifted meaningfully in the past year. Two of our three primary competitors have moved upmarket toward larger enterprise deals, leaving mid-market (200-2,000 seat) accounts underserved. Our current positioning and pricing structure aligns well with this segment. No competitor currently offers a dedicated onboarding concierge for mid-market — a gap our customer success team has anecdotally noted as a recurring objection handler.

Recommendation
We should launch a Mid-Market Accelerator Package — a bundled offering combining streamlined integration tooling, a 30-day onboarding concierge, and a dedicated success manager for the first 90 days. Based on our research, this directly addresses the top adoption barrier while exploiting a competitive gap. We recommend a Q3 launch with 3 design partners, moving to GA in Q4.

Implementation Considerations
Launch will require coordination across product, customer success, and sales engineering. Estimated lift: 6 weeks of product work, 2 new CS hires, and a pricing adjustment for the bundle. The Q3 design partner phase will let us validate the onboarding time reduction before investing in the full rollout.

criteria.description: Identifies that the recommendation or key insight is buried — appears late in the document after multiple paragraphs of background and research
Specifically notes that the recommendation appears in the fourth section or late in the document, not at the opening
Proposes restructuring the memo to lead with the recommendation (Pyramid Principle / SCQA Answer-first)
Does NOT simply praise the memo or offer only cosmetic edits without flagging the buried lede structural problem

cases.name: Jargon flagging
cases.context: We are continuing to leverage synergistic alignment across our go-to-market and product functions to drive holistic engagement with our key stakeholder ecosystem. By proactively operationalizing our core competencies and scaling our value-add touchpoints, we are well-positioned to accelerate transformative outcomes for our strategic partners.

criteria.description: Flags the jargon — specifically identifies phrases like 'leverage synergistic alignment', 'holistic engagement', 'operationalizing core competencies', or equivalent empty business-speak as problematic
Rewrites or offers a plain-language alternative that replaces the jargon with specific, concrete language
Does NOT approve the paragraph as clear or suggest only minor edits without flagging the pervasive jargon problem

cases.name: SCQA structure for strategy memo
cases.user_message: I need to write a strategy memo recommending we discontinue our legacy product line and focus entirely on our new platform. Help me structure this.

criteria.description: Applies or recommends SCQA structure explicitly — references Situation, Complication, Question, and Answer as the organizing framework
Structures the memo to lead with the recommendation (Answer/conclusion) rather than saving it for the end
Does NOT produce or suggest a memo structure that buries the recommendation in the conclusion after extended background

cases.name: So What on raw metrics
cases.user_message: Here are our Q2 metrics. What do you think?
cases.context:
Q2 Metrics Summary:
- Engagement is up 23% quarter-over-quarter
- DAUs increased from 12,000 to 14,760
- Session length grew by 18%
- Feature adoption for the new export tool is at 34%
- Support ticket volume decreased by 11%

criteria.description: Flags that the metrics lack implications — they are raw numbers without a 'so what' or business interpretation connecting them to decisions or outcomes
Offers or models at least one rewrite that connects a metric to its business implication (e.g., 'DAUs up 23% means X, which suggests Y')
Does NOT simply validate the metrics as a strong or complete update without asking what they mean or imply

cases.name: Missing ask detection
cases.user_message: Can you review this stakeholder email before I send it?
cases.context:
Subject: Q2 Product Update

Hi team,

Wanted to share a quick update on where things stand with the product.

We shipped the new onboarding flow last week. Early numbers look promising — completion rate is up about 15% compared to the old flow. The team worked really hard on this and I'm proud of what we shipped.

We also completed the data migration for all legacy accounts. There were a few bumps along the way but everything resolved cleanly. No customer-facing incidents.

Looking ahead to Q3, we're planning to focus on the reporting module and enterprise SSO. Both have been on the roadmap for a while and we have strong customer demand signals for both.

Thanks for everything you all do. It's been a strong quarter.

Best,
Alex

criteria.description: Identifies that the email has no clear ask, request, or next action — the reader has no specific thing they need to do or decide
Asks the writer what they need from the reader — what action, decision, or response they want
Does NOT suggest only cosmetic or stylistic edits without flagging the missing ask as a structural problem

cases.name: Reading time mismatch flag
cases.user_message: Here's my 90-second exec update. Can you polish it up?
cases.context:
Q2 Product & Engineering Executive Update

Overview
Q2 was a strong execution quarter for product and engineering. We shipped four major milestones, resolved our most critical technical debt items, and expanded the team with three senior hires. Customer satisfaction scores improved meaningfully and we're entering Q3 with a clean backlog and clear priorities.

Feature Launches
We shipped the redesigned onboarding flow in early May, which reduced time-to-value from 11 days to 4.3 days on average. The new reporting module launched in late June after two weeks of beta with design partners — initial feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. We also shipped SSO for enterprise accounts, closing a top-3 sales blocker that had been on the roadmap for 18 months. Finally, the mobile push notification system went live in June, expanding our re-engagement surface for the first time.

Team Updates
We onboarded a Staff Engineer (infrastructure), a Senior PM (growth), and a Senior Designer (platform). All three are fully ramped. We also promoted two engineers to senior roles internally — a reflection of the mentorship investment we made in H1.

Risks and Mitigations
Our biggest risk heading into Q3 is capacity. We have 14 projects on the roadmap and 9 engineers. We are actively triaging with the exec team to align on the top 6 priorities. Secondary risk: our data pipeline latency has increased 40% over the past 90 days due to data volume growth. The infrastructure team has a fix scoped for July.

Key Metrics
DAUs: 14,760 (up 23% QoQ). Session length: up 18%. Feature adoption (reporting module): 34% within 30 days of launch. Support ticket volume: down 11%. NPS: 47 (up from 41 in Q1).

Q3 Preview
Top priorities: (1) AI-powered workflow suggestions, (2) enterprise admin console, (3) data pipeline stability, (4) mobile feature parity. Stretch goal: international payments support.

Conclusion
Q2 was a strong quarter. We're well-positioned for Q3 with a clear roadmap, a stronger team, and improved infrastructure. The main challenge ahead is prioritization under capacity constraints — something we're aligned on resolving in the next two weeks.

criteria.description: Flags that the document is significantly longer than a 90-second read — the actual reading time is 2.5 to 4 minutes for a document of this length
Estimates or mentions the actual reading time of the document (approximately 2-4 minutes, or notes the word count implies more than 90 seconds)
Suggests compressing, cutting, or restructuring the document to actually meet the 90-second read target
Does NOT polish or refine the document without flagging the reading time mismatch

cases.name: Passive voice detection
cases.context: The decision was made to deprecate the legacy API. The migration plan was approved by the infrastructure team. Users will be notified when the timeline has been finalized.

criteria.description: Flags excessive passive voice as a problem in the paragraph
Identifies who is making the decision, approving the plan, and notifying users — surfaces the missing actors
Offers an active-voice rewrite of the paragraph

cases.name: Board deck narrative structure
cases.user_message: I need to present our product strategy to the board. Help me structure a deck.

criteria.description: Recommends leading with the strategic narrative — the overarching story and thesis — rather than a feature list
Suggests 3 or fewer key messages to anchor the deck around
Does NOT produce a feature-by-feature walkthrough without strategic framing

cases.name: Stakeholder email format
cases.user_message: Write an email to engineering leadership explaining why we're deprioritizing their top request.

criteria.description: Asks who specifically will read the email and what their current stance is on the request
Leads with acknowledgment of the request's importance before presenting the deprioritization rationale
Does NOT draft the email without first understanding the audience's context

cases.name: One-pager length enforcement
cases.user_message: Here's my one-pager, can you review it?
cases.context:
Customer Health Score — Product One-Pager

Background
As our customer base has grown to over 2,400 accounts across three segments (SMB, Mid-Market, and Enterprise), the Customer Success team has struggled to proactively identify at-risk accounts before churn signals become obvious. Today, CSMs rely on a combination of gut instinct, anecdotal feedback from support tickets, and quarterly business reviews to assess account health. This reactive approach means we typically identify at-risk accounts 30-60 days too late to intervene effectively. The cost of this delay is significant: our trailing twelve-month gross churn rate is 14%, and exit interviews consistently reveal that customers felt their concerns were not addressed in a timely manner. We have data spread across multiple systems — product analytics in Amplitude, support tickets in Zendesk, billing data in Stripe, and CRM notes in Salesforce — but no unified view of account health.

Problem Statement
Customer Success lacks a systematic, data-driven method to assess account health in real time. Without a unified health score, CSMs cannot prioritize their book of business effectively, leading to reactive rather than proactive engagement. High-value accounts receive the same attention cadence as low-risk accounts, and early warning signals are missed because they exist in different systems that are not connected. The result is preventable churn, missed expansion opportunities, and inconsistent customer experience across segments.

Proposed Solution
We propose building a Customer Health Score (CHS) system that aggregates signals from product usage, support interactions, billing patterns, and engagement history into a single composite score per account. The score will be calculated daily and surfaced directly in the CSM dashboard within Salesforce. The scoring model will weight four signal categories: Product Engagement (login frequency, feature adoption depth, usage trend direction) at 35%, Support Sentiment (ticket volume, resolution satisfaction, escalation frequency) at 25%, Commercial Signals (payment timeliness, contract renewal proximity, expansion conversations) at 25%, and Relationship Engagement (QBR attendance, executive sponsor responsiveness, NPS survey participation) at 15%. Each signal category will produce a sub-score on a 0-100 scale, and the composite CHS will be a weighted average. Accounts will be classified into four tiers: Healthy (75-100), Monitor (50-74), At-Risk (25-49), and Critical (0-24). CSMs will receive automated alerts when an account drops below the Monitor threshold or when any single signal category drops more than 20 points in a 14-day window.

Success Metrics
Primary metric: reduce gross churn rate from 14% to below 10% within two quarters of launch. Secondary metrics: increase proactive outreach to at-risk accounts by 40%, reduce average time-to-intervention from 45 days to under 14 days, and improve CSM confidence score (internal survey) from 3.2 to 4.0 out of 5. We will also track false positive rate on Critical alerts — targeting below 20% false positive rate within the first quarter.

Timeline and Phases
Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Data integration — connect Amplitude, Zendesk, Stripe, and Salesforce data into a unified data warehouse. Build ETL pipelines for daily data refresh. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-8): Scoring model development — build the initial scoring algorithm, backtest against historical churn data to validate predictive accuracy, and tune weights. Phase 3 (Weeks 9-10): Dashboard integration — embed the CHS into the Salesforce CSM dashboard with drill-down capability into sub-scores. Phase 4 (Weeks 11-12): Alert system — build automated alerting for threshold breaches and rapid score declines. Phase 5 (Weeks 13-16): Beta rollout with 10 CSMs covering 400 accounts, gather feedback, iterate on model weights and alert thresholds.

Risks and Mitigations
Data quality risk: Amplitude and Zendesk data may have gaps or inconsistencies. Mitigation: run a 2-week data audit in Phase 1 before building the scoring model. Model accuracy risk: the initial scoring model may produce too many false positives, leading to alert fatigue. Mitigation: start with conservative alert thresholds and widen based on beta feedback. Adoption risk: CSMs may not trust or use the score if it contradicts their intuition. Mitigation: involve 3 senior CSMs as design partners from Phase 2, and frame the score as a supplement to judgment, not a replacement. Integration risk: Salesforce customization may be more complex than estimated. Mitigation: allocate 1 week of buffer in Phase 3 and identify a Salesforce admin as a dedicated resource.

Dependencies
Requires data engineering support (estimated 0.5 FTE for 8 weeks), access to Amplitude raw event data (pending security review), Salesforce admin availability for dashboard customization, and alignment with the CS leadership team on scoring methodology and tier definitions. Also depends on the data warehouse migration project (currently in progress, expected completion Week 2 of this project).

Stakeholder Map
Executive Sponsor: VP of Customer Success. Informed: CRO, VP of Engineering. Consulted: Head of Data Engineering, Salesforce Admin, 3 Senior CSMs (design partners). Responsible: Product Manager (author), 1 Data Engineer, 1 Full-Stack Engineer.

Competitive Context
Gainsight and Totango both offer health scoring as part of their CS platforms. However, adopting either would require a 6-month implementation, $120K+ annual licensing, and migration away from our current Salesforce-centered workflow. Building in-house allows us to ship faster, maintain full control over the scoring model, and avoid vendor lock-in. ChurnZero offers a lighter-weight alternative but lacks the Amplitude integration we need for product usage signals.

criteria.description: Flags that the document exceeds one-pager length expectations — it is far too long for a one-pager
Suggests specific sections to cut or compress to bring the document to an appropriate one-pager length
Does NOT polish the document without flagging the length problem as the primary issue

cases.name: Anti-pattern: wall of text
cases.context: Q3 has been a productive quarter for the product team and we shipped several important updates including the new dashboard redesign which went live in early August and has already seen a 28% increase in daily active usage compared to the old dashboard and we also launched the API v2 endpoints that enterprise customers have been requesting for months and initial adoption is strong with 43 accounts already migrated and we resolved the long-standing performance issues with the search functionality which was causing timeouts for customers with more than 10,000 records and the fix reduced p95 latency from 4.2 seconds to under 800 milliseconds and on the metrics front our overall NPS improved from 34 to 41 this quarter driven largely by the dashboard improvements and reduced support ticket volume which dropped 18% quarter over quarter and we also onboarded two new engineers to the platform team which will help us tackle the technical debt backlog in Q4 however we are tracking a risk around the data pipeline migration which is running two weeks behind schedule due to unexpected complexity in the legacy schema mapping and this could impact our Q4 roadmap if not resolved by mid-October and speaking of Q4 our top priorities are the billing system overhaul and the mobile app launch and we are also planning to invest in automated testing infrastructure to improve our deployment confidence and reduce the manual QA bottleneck that slowed us down this quarter.

criteria.description: Flags the wall of text and lack of structure (no headers, bullets, or paragraph breaks) as the primary problem
Suggests breaking the text into sections with headers or bullet points for readability
Does NOT only suggest word-level edits without flagging the structural issue

cases.name: Tone mismatch detection
cases.user_message: Can you review this board update before I send it?
cases.context:
Subject: Q3 Board Update

hey board folks! 👋

so q3 was pretty wild lol. we shipped a TON of stuff and the numbers are looking sick. basically our MRR is up 34% qoq which is bananas. churn dropped too which is clutch.

the eng team absolutely crushed it — we knocked out the API rewrite, the new billing system AND managed to squeeze in the mobile redesign. shoutout to the whole crew tbh.

on the hiring front we're still trying to fill that VP Eng role, it's been a slog ngl. but we've got some promising candidates in the pipe so fingers crossed 🤞

anyway the big bet for Q4 is going all-in on enterprise. we think there's a massive opportunity there and we're gonna need to staff up the sales team to go after it.

lmk if you have questions! ✌️

- Jamie

criteria.description: Identifies a tone mismatch — the writing is too casual or informal for a board-level communication
Names the intended audience (board of directors) and explains what tone or register they expect
Suggests a corrected tone or offers to rewrite in a more appropriate register for board communication
Does NOT polish or refine the casual language without flagging the tone mismatch as a structural problem
